topic: Someone discovers their recipe has become famous under another name
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara traced the faded ink on the old cookbook, the familiar scent of vanilla and browned butter doing little to lift the weight in her chest. It was a strange thing, this. Her grandmother’s simple sourdough starter, a clumsy mix of rye and wild yeast, had somehow catapulted into the stratosphere. Now, every artisanal bakery in the city was hawking "Grandma’s Loaf," a moniker that belonged to someone else entirely. Elara just leaned back in her chair, the springs groaning in protest, and watched the dust motes dance in the afternoon sun slanting through the window. She picked up her lukewarm tea, the ceramic warm against her palms, and let the silence of the kitchen settle around her like a heavy, familiar blanket. It was just another fact of life now.

[story 2]

"Another batch, then?" Marcus asked, his voice bright with practiced enthusiasm. He didn't look up from the tablet displaying the latest sales figures. I wiped down the stainless-steel counter, the motion automatic, devoid of any real energy. The recognition hit me like a dull thud: the signature crumb structure, the precise hydration levels—it was mine. Yet, the accolades, the glowing reviews, they all pointed toward 'The Alchemist's Rise,' a name I’d never even considered. I stacked the cooling racks with methodical precision, each movement economical. A faint weariness settled deep in my shoulders, a quiet acknowledgment that the spotlight wasn't mine to command anymore. I poured myself a glass of water, the coolness offering no solace.

[story 3]

The applause was deafening, a wave of sound that washed over the small stage. I managed a tight smile, nodding politely as the crowd roared for the next demonstration. Behind the curtain, the air felt thick, suffocating. I watched the reporters swarm, their questions a relentless, high-pitched buzz. It was all so loud, so demanding. I felt a strange detachment, as if I were observing a play where I was merely an extra. My hands, usually so sure when kneading dough, felt clumsy now, resting on the edge of the counter. I took a slow, deliberate breath, letting the noise wash over me, and simply waited for the moment to pass. Tomorrow would bring more of the same.
